Coming in as a nontraditional student, I was not sure what to expect, but I found professors who are not only incredibly knowledgeable, but genuinely warm and welcoming. They challenge us with intention. They create an environment where you are stretched, pushed, and sharpened, not for the sake of difficulty, but for the sake of growth.
Being here means engaging with a rigorous curriculum and a community that expects excellence, and it is an honor to rise to that standard every day. I truly love my school. My only hope is that Columbia continues expanding opportunities and funding for nontraditional students like me, because we bring a unique strength, perspective, and resilience that enrich the Columbia community.

This school is absolutely amazing. If accepted you win twice; a top school in America's top city. This school is unmatched in academics, student body, and student life. Probably the best all-around school in America. Top departments in every subject. Endless course catalogue. The campus is beautiful, safe, and the classes are great. No fluff classes here to fill requirements, all academic. The Core classes are probably the best classes a student would ever take anywhere.

It is the most amazing universty i've been so far
i studied Politics there and it was Holy
Like as i gifted student its a pleasure to meet the professors and the Staff who support me
Thanks Columbia university for making my dream come true
Only been here a month but very good first impressions overall, owing to the impeccable dining, supportive professors, and safe campus.

As a transfer student, I can say that Columbia is a big school in a big city. All the professors I had the opportunity to interact seem accesible and patient. Although in the community college I was before all professors were closed to their students, here at Columbia, most of the professors are opened to listen and talk to every student. Of course the amount of students in the classroom doesn't help at the moment to interact with the professors. The food they serve is delicious, diverse and the meal plan make it cheap. The only problem about the campus is that isn't specifically safe after 11:00 pm. But at least we receive emails every time that any incident happen, even though those incidents doesn't affect students.

I’m an older, non-traditional student who never imagined an Ivy League education was within my reach. Despite my age, the social student life experience has been incredible. With numerous student groups and a wide range of interests and cultural experiences, I find numerous ways to relax between the demanding rigor of classes. The opportunities for networking with alumni, companies, and others are endless. While Columbia does have its institutional and bureaucratic challenges, which are to be expected for any institution of its age, the education and opportunities are of the highest quality. I’m incredibly proud to be a Columbia Lion.
Columbia is a great school overall, even though recently there has been a lot of upheaval and overall division. I think generally the student body has a good view of each other and their programs.

ai_summary: Cornell University is frequently praised for its beautiful campus and extensive academic opportunities , fostering a balanced "work-hard play-hard" environment. Many students appreciate the diverse community and the wealth of resources available to explore various interests. While academics are challenging and rigorous, students often highlight the dedication of professors and the stimulating coursework that prepares them for future endeavors. However, some express concerns about the high cost of attendance and the competitive atmosphere, which can lead to stress and isolation if one doesn’t find their niche. The social scene is vibrant, with plenty of extracurricular activities and events, although some feel that involvement in Greek life is often emphasized. Overall, students recognize that while Cornell has its challenges, it offers a distinctive and enriching college experience.

reviews.text: Office of Academic Discovery and Impact, and my major advisor are the main reasons I was able to succeed during my undergraduate career. Finding your support system is the most important thing to have a great experience, in my opinion. Times may get tough, but a support system is invaluable to keep moving forward.

I appreciate all of the resources available to students and have had wonderful professors. I don't love paying for laundry, having to pay for a bus pass after year one, or the hours of the dining halls (earliest they open is 7, most close by 8:30).

Overall, my experience has been really great. I feel very safe on campus and love all the academic and health opportunities like the tutors, libraires, courses, and gyms, dining options and walkability. However, it is not financially comforting to come here as tuition is awfully expensive and not very realistic.

Cornell is a great place for the arts and sciences academically. Its housing could be improved and tuition/financial aid needs to be more clear. However, there are a lot of awesome student-run organizations where I've found my place.

We like Ezra Cornell promised “any person … any study” to students of the University he founded.
What could be improved- Cornell teaches the principles of Kaizen to strive for continuous improvement.

Although chemical science was my primary aspiration, my experience at Cornell was made completely through music. Playing in Cornell's Chamber Orchestra, for instance, kept my musical interests as a violist thriving. In addition, I was able to embrace my Chinese background at Cornell, whether that be through the Chinese Students Association, whose hot pot nights were genuinely too good to pass up, or the Cornell-China and Asia-Pacific Studies (CAPS) Program, where I gained invaluable internship experience studying abroad in Beijing. Overall, Cornell helped me solidify the kind of impact I hope to make on the world, both as a scientist and a student.

I transferred to Cornell my sophomore year. I thought the Office of Undergraduate Biology did a good job at integrating their transfer students. My overall experience was pretty good; classes were interesting, the campus was nice, there were lots of clubs/organizations to join, and the food was good! There are not a ton of dining options on central campus - they're mostly by the dorms which are more peripheral. You'll get your steps in for sure :D It does get very grey in the winter, but when the sun comes out, you'll see EVERYONE outside on the slope or on the quad trying to soak up the rays. I found lots of awesome study spots around campus in random buildings - one of my favorite places to study was MVR. The surrounding nature is amazing. I have to recommend walking around campus with no destination in mind! The Commons was a nice area with lots of good food. Apple Fest down in the Commons was one of the best fall activities!

My favorite aspect of Cornell was its location in a small town. Being somewhat isolated made it much easier to build a close-knit community, and I felt more connected to the people around me than I would have in a large city. I also appreciated how flexible the curriculum was — I was able to take classes across different colleges and explore interests outside my primary field, which made my academic experience feel more personalized and well-rounded.

Overall, it's a pretty good environment that provides the things you need for your future and present. School is pretty big and definitely has some issues of its own but I would say its pros easily overcome the cons.

Overall good experience, but very expensive. I spent a lot of time working in order to pay for it, which affected my grades and overall experience at the school. I also met lifelong friends and received opportunities I never would have elsewhere.

Cornell University has been an incredibly rewarding place to start my college journey. As a Black freshman football player studying biological sciences, I expected a challenging workload, and my first semester definitely pushed me academically. I finished with a 3.0 GPA, which I am proud of, especially balancing academics and athletics. One of the highlights of my experience has been the support from my professors, who are approachable, understanding, and genuinely willing to help students succeed. I’ve also loved the variety of food options on campus, the party scene, the diversity of the student body, and the strong sense of community among students. Being part of the football team has helped me build close relationships and feel connected to campus life. However, I would like to see improved meal rotation in the dining halls and renovations to some freshman dorms. Overall, Cornell has been a challenging yet fulfilling environment that has helped me grow academically and personally.

As an animal science student, Cornell has a lot of great resources and programs in their department. Lots of research opportunities and hands-on experiences for students. Ithaca is nicest in the summer when nobody else is here.

The academics are amazing at Cornell, professors are some of the best I've ever had. The large lecture classes are a bit disengaging but the discussion classes and smaller upper level courses make up for it. I would like to see smaller classes though even at lower levels implemented throughout a variety of majors and subjects. The food is okay, much better than much other college cafeteria food, and large in diversity, but lacking in terms of healthiness and taste sometimes. My dorm as a transfer is not as nice as I would hope, having been a recently converted frat house that was just recently underwent renovation this summer but many of my friends have much nicer dorms with in-house laundry and dining halls.
pretty good! the experience has been very enriching and i have gone to europe with the chorus and sang in an acapella group

I love Cornell University. From my professors, to the people who help at resource centers, to Cornell Health, everyone here is committed to supporting and lifting up students. The campus and city is beautiful.

ai_summary: Dartmouth College offers a unique blend of rigorous academics and a tight-knit community, making it stand out among Ivy League institutions. Reviews highlight the exceptional quality of education , with small class sizes and dedicated professors who prioritize undergraduate learning, fostering an environment of collaboration rather than competition. The campus is celebrated for its beauty and outdoor opportunities, appealing to those who enjoy nature, though its rural location can feel isolating. The social scene often revolves around Greek life and campus events, which some find overwhelming if they don't align with mainstream activities. While many students appreciate the strong support systems and networks available, some challenges exist, particularly for those from less rigorous academic backgrounds, as they may feel a significant adjustment is needed. Overall, students express a deep sense of belonging and community, contributing to a transformative college experience .

reviews.text: You can find wonderful research and career opportunities in Dartmouth. Many professors are willing to do much to help struggling students and would like to get to know them better. Not all incoming students are used to the academic rigor, but there are supportive professors who can help. Although the people here are nice for the most part, there is still some racism or bullying within the community. This particularly concerns the Digital Communications Manager of the Hop; while coaching the crew team at her last school, she unfairly restricted active participation for certain students who didn't satisfy her biases, harshly punished them for minor rule-breaking that she herself and other students were not above doing and used it as an excuse to kick them off the team, and was also involved in slandering such students to get them in more trouble. Dartmouth should really do some research on the people they consider hiring and not just assume any of their alumni is a good person.

Very strong alumni network with amazing traditions such as the freshman trips and the bonfire/winter carnivals. Overall amazing once in a lifetime experience that I will remember before going back to the city.

Dartmouth is an incredibly special place! The level of commitment you get from your professors is unmatched by other institutions. Although it can feel small at times, it's size offers great benefits. It is very easy to win grants and other forms of funding for your any research, club, or event you want to pursue. The professors are nearly always open to get a meal with you, and genuinely care about your success. The student culture is very welcoming and friendly. No matter where you are on campus there is always somebody around that would love to learn your name and sit and chat. Every day is work hard, play hard. Us Dartmouth students are very committed to our studies and reaching our career goals, but at the same time we want to live it up—whether thats skiing at "The Dartmouth Skiway," going to a local farmers market, playing pong at one of the fraternities, or even taking a weekend road trip to Boston or Montreal.

Prospective of a parent/alum: there are lots of opportunities, clubs, frats/sororities, career etc. However, competition is the key word here. Every step of the way you have to compete and be better than the next in line. Meaningful Club? Need to prove you are the best. Not just: why don’t I try badminton or consulting? Doesn’t fly. Also true about frats: need to do lots of prep and KNOW in advance. Career opportunities are unparallel and the career office is super supportive. But you are fighting for a few amazing positions against a highly potent pool. Should be perfect. Bottom line: you fill fight the odds to get there, but competition will just intensify then.

I was really impressed with the professors especially, I never imagined that I could take a professor out to lunch or even talk and discuss their works. The research was always there always hands on, though the social scene was a bit hierarchical... But it was an unforgettable experience

I am currently a student at Dartmouth and I love it. It is very challenging and will test the resilience of its students, but the payoff is worth it. There truly is something for everyone here. The area surrounding the school is small but beautiful. Housing can be kind of a tossup--there are great residence halls and also some pretty bad ones. The food is generally good, but the options are limited. There is a group of people for everyone and I have met some truly amazing people here.
BEST SCHOOL EVER. I have never seen such well rounded individuals in every facet of life ever in my life! Regardless of the ranking Dartmouth is best.

COME TO DARTMOUTH!! Never in my life have I met such incredible, down to earth, loving people. As a non legacy non athlete on a full ride scholarship, I never felt alienated ONCE- people are so lovely here. There is a REASON I hear so many people choose here over MIT, Harvard, etc: we are actually happy here. Party life is amazing, academics are engaging and incredible, and I have never been happier.
Some advice!!!
1. BUY IN!! Dartmouth almost feels like a summer camp at first, complete with silly traditions, crazy dances, and pranks. It is SO MUCH FUN if you roll with it and completely buy in :)
2. Join things!!! I joined acapella here- best decision of my entire life. I have already made lifelong friends across all years!
3. Take it easy first term. I’m premed, but am only taking one STEM class in order to get used to the colleges pacing. Its manageable don’t worry!!
Can’t wait to see you on the green ❤️

It is a very lovely environment that facilitates higher learning. There are plenty of opportunities to join clubs, sports teams, and get internships throguht the college. Most of the professors are research professors so they understand the struggles studnets have to go through and are easy going as long as you talk to them. Greek life is great, very inclusive with gender inclusive housing options as well as the traditional fraternity and sorority. Networking optiosn are outstanding too with a lot of people that you can talk to about furthering your ideas on a profession

Dartmouth appeals to a specific type of person. As an Ivy League school, the College offers a lot of institutional funding for student's education, faculty, and experiences both on and off campus. The quaint New England setting is in one of the most picturesque parts of the country and it offers a kind of quiet that allows you to focus on your studies. If you're looking for a party school in a big city, this isn't it.

Dartmouth has more than earned its prestige as the school is wonderful in nearly all aspects. The professors are excellent and top of their field, the students are engaged and interesting, and just about every experience you could hope to have would be covered by the activities on offer.

As someone who didn't go to an elite private high school, the academics at Dartmouth have felt daunting. I have constantly felt behind compared to my peers and it is reflected in my grades, unfortunately. No matter how hard I study in certain science classes, I tend to fall either at or below median due to the fact that over 70% of them have a stronger background. Dartmouth's STEM classes are really not accessible for middle/lower class students. Most STEM professors are unhelpful. The social sciences at Dartmouth are phenomenal. As someone who is an Anthropology major, doing research in Anth, and premed, I have to say that the Anth Dept is supportive and accessible, while most of the premed classes/profs are out of touch and don't care to bridge the knowledge gap. I am grateful for the opportunity to engage in Anth research with renowned professors and have found a great, intelligent, and uplifting community!!

I love the small community and the people from many different cultures. I like my dorm right across from the center of campus and its proximity to the football fields, where I train every day. I like the food options available and the student events on the weekends.

Dartmouth is simply the most wonderful undergraduate experience I could imagine.
I’m from a large city and attended a public high school where people rarely went off to schools like Dartmouth. When I first arrived on campus, I was nervous. Since then, I’ve found an incredibly beautiful and caring community made up of remarkable individuals. Dartmouth has everything you’d expect it to: incredible faculty, small, (sometimes extremely) rigorous classes, excellent facilities, and an endless number of academic and career opportunities. It also has a many things you wouldn’t expect: its own skiway, outdoor lodges, countless traditions, and a very active social life, to name a few. The result is an experience where you live and learn in an incredibly enriching community, all in the pristine backdrop of the Upper Valley. In every sense, it lives up to its ascribed ideal of the “College on the Hill”.

Amazing academic and social college experience. There is always work to be done to improve any institution, but it is very possible to find academic support, funding for opportunities you are passionate about, and great friends here.

ai_summary: Harvard University offers a dynamic and diverse academic environment , characterized by rigorous courses and distinguished faculty who engage students in meaningful discussions. Many reviews highlight the campus's beautiful architecture and convenient resources, including libraries and student centers, which foster collaboration and community. While the academic challenge is noted as significant, students appreciate the opportunities for personal growth and networking that come with being part of such a prestigious institution. Conversely, some reviews point to a less vibrant social scene , with concerns about inclusivity for marginalized groups and a perceived sense of elitism on campus. Overall, Harvard attracts students seeking both academic excellence and a unique college experience, though navigating its social landscape can be complex.

reviews.text: It was a good school with strong academic programs, social life, and on-campus housing. Appreciated the diversity in student population as well

When I first applied to this school, I was elated. It had always been a fantasy of mine to attend THE Harvard University. until I realized that my dream had became a NIGHTMARE. Each night I would be awoken from my sleep by the feeling of dirty RATS clambering all over me. The worst part? One of the rats had RABIES and I had to go to the DOCTOR and get a rabies shot: which led to the continuation of my SEVERE mental health issues and SEWERSLIDAL thoughts. Thinking about coming here? Thinking about relishing in the utter prestige of the university. You're better off going to COMMUNITY COLLEGE. In fact, you're better off packing all your bags, and signing up for MILITARY SERVICE. World war III here I come! ANYTHING is better than Harvard. At least on the battlefields of Iran I won't have to get a RABIES SHOT.
It was aight. .

> “My overall experience at Harvard University has been life‑changing. The academic rigor and world‑renowned faculty challenged me to expand my intellectual horizons every day. I especially appreciated the cutting‑edge research facilities and the vibrant student organizations that foster leadership and innovation. The diverse community made the campus feel like a global hub, enriching both my social and academic life. On the flip side, I felt the course registration system could be more flexible for interdisciplinary interests, and there’s room to improve mentorship programs for graduate students. Despite these tweaks, Harvard equipped me with skills and confidence to pursue ambitious goals. I recommend prospective students to embrace the intense environment and make the most of the unparalleled

It is a nice school with lots of mind blowing structures. Based on my analysis, it has produced a set of brilliant students who are out there appreciating the good instil of discipline into them which made them what they're now

My overall experience with Harvard University has been outstanding. The academic environment is challenging and inspiring, with professors who are leaders in their fields and truly invested in student success. Campus resources, from libraries to research labs, are world-class and always accessible. The diverse student body creates a vibrant, supportive community where collaboration is encouraged and new ideas are welcomed. There are countless opportunities for involvement, whether through clubs, events, or internships. Harvard not only pushed me to excel academically, but also helped me grow personally and professionally. I feel truly privileged to be a part of such an amazing institution.

Everything about Harvard was wonderful, except for the administration and the people in charge of running the school. They could do a lot better at adjusting policies with the times, listening to student advocates and their feedback, and taking care of the student body's mental health. They simply worry too much about their statistics and liability. Otherwise, the professors, the people, the plethora of diverse classes, and the housing/campus life is amazing!

My experience at Harvard University has been a mix of awe, growth, challenge, and perspective. It’s one of those places where the mythology surrounding it - the prestige, the legacy, the brand meets reality in unexpected ways. Yes, it lives up to its reputation for excellence, but what most people don’t realise is that Harvard’s real strength isn’t its name, its red brick buildings, or even its professors. It’s the ecosystem a living, breathing network of ambitious, curious, and sometimes intimidatingly accomplished people who push you to think, question, and stretch yourself every single day. Harvard isn’t just a college; it’s almost like an experiment in human potential. You walk into classrooms where debates feel like mini-United Nations sessions, dining halls buzz with the next startup pitch, and hallways echo with the sound of students juggling 10 ideas at once. It’s inspiring and exhausting at the same time like trying to drink from a firehose, but somehow loving the chaos of it.

My experience with Harvard University has been highly inspiring and academically enriching. The intellectual environment pushes students to think critically, explore interdisciplinary ideas, and aim for excellence. Professors are deeply knowledgeable and encourage independent thinking, while the resources, libraries, and research opportunities are exceptional. The diverse student community offers exposure to different perspectives from around the world. However, the academic pressure can be intense at times, and better mental health support and workload balance would improve the overall experience. Overall, Harvard provides an unmatched learning environment that prepares students for leadership and innovation.
It was way too easy. i felt that it couldve have been easier and harder. some classes i felt were so freaking challenging and others were so easy.
As a graduate student, I feel a bit left out. The school definitely prioritizes undergrads. My friend at Yale has a much better experience with the grad school there than I have had here
Most amazing university in the world. Best Ivy League I have attended after Columbia. Especially HMS.

The Aspire Leadar program was my amazing experience I took part in All activities of the program, during my two months there, I worked with people who were in the leadership program. So I could tell you a little about what it's like. This program provides us opportunity of Networking with such students who are away from my country. The amazing thing for me is that the Indian and Pakistani interconnect through this program without discrimination as the Students of one platform. Further Sometimes you are given cross business rotations so you learn how to work in different industries and environments,During my short time as an intern I got to work with some brilliant people and I got an insight into the actual management of an MNC. It is such exposure that trains the employees to face real world situations in the industry, something that isn't given too much attention during undergraduate studies At least this program they did take students who had just finished their graduations

I did a semester as a undergraduate. Besides it being "Harvard" I actually liked it alot. Though it was hard socially. You need to apply and go to interviews for almost every club. So it was hard to make a friendgroup. Though maybe it would be different if a spent a longer time here, idk.

My experience with Harvard University has been both challenging and rewarding. The CORe program is providing a rigorous, practical introduction to business fundamentals, and the case-based learning made the material highly applicable to real-world scenarios.
What I valued most is engaging with a diverse global cohort. The discussions offered fresh perspectives and deepened my learning. The platform itself was intuitive, and the teaching quality lived up to Harvard’s reputation.
If I could suggest one improvement, it would be to expand networking opportunities beyond discussion boards—perhaps through regional meetups or virtual sessions with faculty. This would make the experience feel even more connected to Harvard’s wider community.
Overall, CORe is an excellent experience that I would recommend to anyone seeking to strengthen their business knowledge while connecting with professionals worldwide.

ai_summary: Princeton University is widely recognized for its strong focus on academics and offers a challenging curriculum that pushes students to excel while providing ample resources for support. Many reviews highlight the dedication of professors and the extensive financial aid available, ensuring that diverse student backgrounds are well-supported. Although the academic environment is intense and can be stressful, especially with the culture of grade deflation, students appreciate the collaborative atmosphere and strong sense of community that develops among peers. Student life is vibrant, with numerous clubs and activities, though some note that socializing can sometimes feel cliquish or isolating, particularly for newcomers. The campus is noted for its beauty and safety, and the surrounding town of Princeton adds to the overall experience. While there are concerns about the pressure to succeed and the social scene, many reviewers still regard their time at Princeton as transformative and fulfilling , emphasizing the unique combination of academic rigor and personal growth that the university fosters.

reviews.text:
Princeton would rather pay full tuition for 5800 undergraduates at around $394,000,000 than lose 8 percent of its endowment to taxes and around $3,000,000,000.
At the end of fiscal 2025, the university’s endowment was valued at $35.7 billion, with a payout for the year of $1.8 billion. That represents a spending rate of a little over 5%, according to the university
PRINCO thought they could outsmart the general market in private equities.
The cuts to services and staff benefits are not important to Princeton as it guards its endowment from impact.
Students receiving full aid and yes, even those with family incomes of $250,0000 are pleased. Pell Grant admits are up not due to generosity but from greed of the business that is Princeton.

Princeton University claims to have one of the best computer science departments yet cannot update its own university software for an efficient meal swap program? “The decision (to decommission the meal swap system) was made by the University. However, we were told that the meal exchange software is antiquated and that due to the cost cutting initiative, the University is not in a position to fund the upgrade,” chairman of the Graduate Interclub Council wrote in a statement to The Daily Princetonian.

Max Weiss, a Princeton professor of history and an outspoken advocate of an academic boycott of Is real, led a walkout of his History of Pales tine /Israe l class into McCosh Courtyard, where an anti-Isra el encampment was established. He taught his lesson from there, making him the first faculty member to lecture from the encampment, the Princeton Alumni Weekly reported. Weiss told the publication he hopes the illegal encampment will one day be commemorated with "a plaque."

9 undergraduates and 3 graduate students since 2022 is a concerning number of students. Student athletes are given baseline concussion studies. Princeton students need pre-admission mental health evaluations to match. The atmosphere is not for everyone.

Princeton Grad Student Treasurer Challenged to Subtract From 10,000.
On Monday, the Graduate Student Government (GSG) released the Budget and Finance Report for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, which revealed that it has already maxed out or exceeded its annual budget in three categories.
Specifically, Events Board contributions, a body in the GSG offering funding for Graduate Student Events, has already exhausted its $10,000 allotment. Special Events — which covers alcohol purchases, materials for events, and catering services — and the Ivy+ Leadership Summit overshot their budgets by $1,476.84 and $823.60, respectively.
On top of these expenses, the report also noted that of the $23,625.00 the GSG had spent on “Beer Jackets,” only $10,519.75 had been recovered in sales.

From the 2025 Report from the Treasurer: “The University is potentially facing a new 8 percent endowment tax, which applies to universities with endowments of over $2 million per student and a tuition-paying population of at least 3,000. With a projected increase of financial aid spending to $327 million in 2025–26, there is a possibility that the University will avoid the endowment tax by having under 3,000 tuition-paying students. The University has previously declined to comment on the endowment tax and the number of students that pay tuition.”
Student life could be improved, but the academics are very comprehensive. All of the courses I have taken so far are interesting.

Thank you to the professors who filed for my Statements for an A+. I fully appreciate this extra effort by professors and I am grateful that my work has been recognized as deserving of the honor at Princeton University!

Princeton University’s President defends higher education on free speech yet the university fails in hiring and admissions to increase free diverse speech.
Eisgruber said universities often reflect what is happening in the culture. "It's on campuses because it's in society," Eisgruber replied. "we are having trouble talking to one another across political differences. Campuses are part of that society, and they're a place where civil discussions and discussions across difference are especially important." When Professor Robert George retires there will be groupthink because of the limited diversity of faculty or students regarding viewpoint. If you are not far left you can expect to keep your views to yourself or face ostracism by faculty and peers. The majority of Princeton graduates self-report that they plan to stay in academia continuing the cycle.

At Princeton you can elect to take a junk course like “Bad Girls.” If you are not paying tuition perhaps you do not mind wasting your time on similar junk courses. If you are a paying tuition I hope you are taking quality courses that will benefit your growth and future employment outside academia.

Admissions standards are not as expected and are varied. One student aced the most rigorous courses in high school, AP 5s, SAT 1590. Another student in the same class had an SAT of 1300 and does not even know something basic as calculating the consumer surplus as an area. The best and the brightest do not opt for test optional nor wish to stand on a soapbox as an activist instead of being a serious academic scholar. Brilliant peers should be admitted for an entire class with a basic expectation of a high level of academic achievement. Grade inflation is a disservice to all students and acceptances not based on merit devalues a diploma’s worth and the reputation of the university.

A new University policy will ban recording on campus with few exceptions starting Jan. 1, raising serious concerns for journalists, among others. It was passed by a wide margin at the Nov. 10 meeting of the Council of the Princeton University Community (CPUC), with no opposing votes.
The policy, which is slightly longer than a page, “prohibits the installation or use of any device for listening, observing, photographing, recording, amplifying, transmitting, or broadcasting sounds or events occurring in any place where the individual or group involved has a reasonable expectation of being free from unwanted surveillance, eavesdropping, recording, or observation without the knowledge and consent of all participants subject to such recordings.” Public or open meetings also may be covered by the no-recording policy “when it has been explicitly stated that recording is not permitted.”

New Princeton students were met with a table of condoms during orientation for an “I Love Female Orgas m” presentation. The students, particularly young women seeking to further their education do not rank being receptacles as top priority of their education.

Several recent incidents of antisemetic graffiti on campus and around town. Where is campus security as the defacing of property occurs? There are cameras everywhere so why haven’t they been identified and caught? Hate is tolerated by inaction. Yet protestors are protected by campus security and town police as they shut down roads and march through Princeton.

“Here is the statement on disruption from the administration — touted as an improvement.
“Going forward, attendees at University events should expect a single warning not to disrupt an event or prevent an invited guest from speaking. Disruptive behavior may result in immediate removal from the event and disciplinary action or other consequences.”
Let me see if I can crack the code. “A single warning” means “one free shot” to disrupt a speaker. “Should expect” translates to “or maybe more, it depends.” And “disruptive behavior may result in ...” signals passivity.
You are either serious about protecting speech or you are not serious. A serious administration might have communicated something like this: “Those who disrupt a speaker will be immediately removed and may face additional consequences.”

ai_summary: The University of Pennsylvania offers a mix of rigorous academics and vibrant student life , making it an attractive option for many high school seniors. Reviewers appreciate the beautiful campus and the diverse range of academic programs, particularly highlighting opportunities in research and cross-disciplinary studies. Many students find the competitive environment both motivating and stressful, often describing a "work hard/play hard" culture that heavily emphasizes pre-professionalism. While the professors are generally praised for their expertise and accessibility, some departments, like math, have faced criticism. Social life at Penn is lively, with numerous events and a strong Greek presence, though the dining options and housing conditions receive mixed reviews. Students express a desire for better mental health support and inclusivity initiatives, indicating areas for potential improvement. Overall, Penn provides a robust educational experience enriched by a culturally dynamic setting in Philadelphia.

reviews.text: Top tier professors. The quality of my education here has far exceeded other colleges and universities I have attended. They are a little but under-resourced for fully online students, but they are growing in that capacity every semester.

I'm not sure if I would attend again because of it's very pre-professional environment. I didn't feel like I was there to learn but rather get a job. I will say, the name recognition of Penn and Wharton has really helped me later on as well.

I love the scene and the sense of community at Penn. The people I've met here are genuinely some of the most interesting and kind individuals. My department is also a highlight—it feels very tight-knit and offers amazing resources for students.

The University of Pennsylvania embodies the perfect balance of “work hard, play hard,” supported by an exceptional network that lasts a lifetime. Its rich traditions and Ivy League legacy create a campus culture where ambition and community thrive together. Every school at Penn offers world‑class academics, with Wharton widely regarded as the best business school in the world. Penn’s dual‑degree options—especially the M&T Program, often considered the most elite undergraduate program in the country—set students up for unparalleled long‑term success. Combined with vibrant student life, spirited athletics, and a genuinely fun college experience, Penn stands out as a truly extraordinary place to grow.
Great academics, social life, city life, and a work hard play hard balance. If you love exploring the city and always having something to do you will love Penn

The University university of Pennsylvania is overall a rigorous institution that maintains Ivy League standards in terms of quality of education, environment, and student experience. The university has lots of resources for almost anything that a student can need, whether it be professional outreach, financial assistance, food assistance, and many other benefits. I find it catering all classes of people. However, some may find its diversity a bit lacking. If it’s your first time trying to get there, you might get confused since it’s in the middle of nowhere. But once you step onto campus, you’ll fall in love with it. If you can handle the extreme workload of multiple papers and assignments every few days as well as the tough grading, then UPenn might be the one for you.
I haven't started yet but from my research university of Pennsylvania is an esteemed university that trains leaders those aspiring to be great with all love, patience and equality

UPenn has provided me with more resources and opportunities than I could ever imagine. It seems like every day I learn about a new service offered at Penn. If I ever had an idea I wanted to pursue (which I do, in fact, have many) I'm assured that there are ample opportunities at Penn to help me bring it to fruition. On top of that, the social scene is amazing here. All the faculty are dedicated towards making sure each student is having a great time and is making friends. Many events are hosted each day where we can cool down and socialize after a long day of classes. It's great, I'm having TONS of fun here while being super intellectually stimulated!

My favorite part of attending Penn is its connections to the Philadelphia community. There are many tutoring services that Penn offers to West Philadelphia elementary and middle schools that allow students to gain a sense of what Philadelphia is like outside of campus. Clubs as well, such as Penn Wellness, serve the community of Philadelphia with food and fun events to help provide access to the public's needs. I would like to see change in healthy food options on campus. Penn offers great options for dining halls, but nutritious food is not always available, nor is variety is food that is offered.
Great university and great campus. People here are fascinating and energetic, love the professors here. But Philly needs to be improved
Good school overall. Campus food needs a lot of work. The professors in the STEM department are not the best, and there is definitely grade deflation. But everything else is amazing.
Penn is the right fit for me- I wanted a "work hard, play hard" place with top notch academics but also a vibrant social life!

My experience at the University of Pennsylvania has been both academically challenging and personally enriching. As a student in the architecture program, I’ve had the opportunity to work closely with passionate faculty, collaborate on complex design projects, and engage in coursework that pushes me to think critically about sustainability, space, and social impact. The diverse campus community has exposed me to new perspectives and helped me grow both as a designer and a global citizen.

As an international student, I’ve had a positive experience at Penn. The school proactively supports students through Campus Express and helpful orientation sessions. I appreciated learning early on about Penn’s safe, accessible transportation and the Career Center’s services—resume reviews, mock interviews, and career advising. Advisors and staff respond promptly, and when visa delays impacted many international students this year, the school provided detailed emails, info sessions, and online course options to keep us on track. However, I hope Penn can offer more scholarships for international students, as tuition is high and financial aid limited. Additionally, while many offices respond efficiently, some departments are understaffed, leading to long delays. Finally, on-campus job opportunities are limited, making it difficult for international students with work restrictions to support themselves. More positions and support for student employment would make a meaningful difference.
Work hard, play hard environment. Help is always available but must be sought out. Philly is a great city!

ai_summary: Yale University offers a compelling mix of rigorous academics and a vibrant student life , appealing to a diverse student body. Many reviews emphasize the supportive community fostered by the residential college system, which encourages close bonds among students and promotes engagement across various extracurricular activities. The professors are frequently noted for their dedication to student success, providing both academic support and mentorship, although some reviews highlight inconsistencies in teaching quality within certain departments, particularly in STEM. Students appreciate the extensive resources available, including peer tutoring and a wide array of clubs and activities, but note the workload can be demanding. The campus is described as both beautiful and inviting, with options for energetic or tranquil environments. While most students cherish their experience, some initial culture shock and social dynamics are noted, especially for those from underrepresented backgrounds. Overall, Yale is recognized for its rich academic culture and strong sense of community, making it a unique and rewarding place to study.

reviews.text: Academics are tough, but there is plenty of support - you just need to ask. My favorite part of Yale is that everyone here takes their academics seriously and I have access to so many opportunities and professors at the top of their field. , but there are also plenty of people like me who also are very social.

This is my first semester at Yale and I am incredibly impressed by all that this school offers. Excellent academics, student life, and endless resources. The infrastructure they have in place for students in terms professional and academic development is quite impressive. As a student, you really feel like you have everything at your fingertips to succeed and thrive, and endless opportunities to explore ideas. The campus is incredible, such beautiful architecture and facilities, and not to mention they cook great food.
The community at Yale is unlike anything I've ever seen before. People here are so kind. I truly feel at home here.
I love it. The classes, professors, academic, extracurricular and professional opportunities are unmatched. The school has the best people and is so much fun and fulfilling.

A few months ago, a friend invited me to sit in on Professor Marci Shore's class, "European Intellectual History Since Nietzsche." After her lecture on Heidegger, I shared an amusing conversation with her students:
"I feel like Instagram is the modern-day Das-man."
"Totally—do you think AI can experience Dasein?"
And as we laughed over lunch about whether Olivia Rodrigo was "authentic" or "inauthentic" by Heidegger's definition, I realized Yalies don't compromise breadth for depth. Obtaining a liberal arts education within a research university framework, I have the unique opportunity to become both a scientist and a citizen. As a student, I'm surrounded by passionate, intellectually and ideologically diverse students at every moment, which makes thought-provoking discussions inevitable.

Attending Yale has been a great opportunity for me. I am from a small town so I've been able to meet people from all different walks of life going to school at Yale. The academic rigor at the school is also definitely present and I've had to change as a student to best fit the academic challenge in my classes.

I've learned that Yale feels a lot smaller than other colleges, for better or worse. It makes for a more intimate college experience, but if you want to really explore and get out into the world, it might not be for you. There are obviously so many resources available to you, but it still feels supported by alumni than other colleges like Harvard and MIT.

Yale has been the best experience of my life. I love the residential college system. The professors are amazing. There are so many activities to do on campus. Nothing but great things to say about my life at Yale and can’t wait for the rest of my time there.
I liked all the resources that were available for first year students. The residential colleges make you feel like you have a family on campus

Yale was a great experience for me as an undergraduate student. I had an excellent academic experience, studying physics and history. I made lifelong friends and tried to make the best of all of the amazing opportunities I was offered through the university!

Great community spirit.
Breadth of courses
All inclusive and welcoming community
Great gym facilities

What I love most about Yale is the access to world-class resources and the inspiring community of peers and professors. The faculty are genuinely invested in students’ success, and the residential college system creates a close-knit, supportive environment.

The overall experience is good. The vibe is very friendly. Everyone is busy but nobody shows off how much they are doing. People genuinely see each other as individual humans and treat each other with kindness.

Positive and a good start to college life. I found the campus to be safe and inviting. I liked how I was able to walk in one direction and find quiet and quaint spaces. If I walked in the other direction I was able to be in the heart of the city. It allowed someone like me to get a mix of high energy or quiet campus life.

being part of the Yale Young Global Scholar from this summer I really appreciate the diversity Yale brings to this campus and the university as a whole. The professors and students that helped with this program have been very welcoming and have showed me a different way of thinking. Showing us the life of a student and lecturing us about interesting topics you would not get taught in high school, Yale has been a great influence in my life.
